title: Carrot and orange soup
subtitle: Bright, sunlit, and sweetly acidic
excerpt: Carrots blended with fresh orange juice and a little coriander into a vivid, sweet soup that is lighter and more refreshing than a plain carrot soup. Can be served warm or chilled. Ready in 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Carrot and orange soup became a British restaurant and home-cooking staple from the 1980s onwards. The combination of carrot sweetness with citrus acid is a technique borrowed from French nouvelle cuisine. No single canonical source.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before adding the orange juice. Keeps for up to three months. Add fresh orange juice when reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to two days ahead. Add fresh orange juice on the day of serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: Orange zest (the coloured outer rind) adds aromatic citrus oils and flavour without acidity. Orange juice adds sweetness, acid, and moisture. In soups, adding both gives full citrus flavour; adding only juice gives brightness; adding only zest gives fragrance without changing the colour.

content.text: The difference between carrot and orange soup and a plain carrot soup is the brightness of the citrus. Orange juice added after blending rather than during cooking keeps its fresh, acidic quality: cooked citrus becomes muted and loses its point. A little
added with the carrots carries the orange flavour through the soup, while the juice at the end provides the lift. This is one of the few vegetable soups that works as well cold as it does warm.

items.prepNote: zest of 1, juice of both (about 150 ml)

content.text: Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a large saucepan over a medium-low heat. Add the {{onion}} onion and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Add the {{garlic}} of garlic and {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander and cook for 1 minute.
Add the {{carrot}} of chopped carrots and the zest of {{orange}} of orange. Pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot stock. Bring to a simmer and cook for 20 minutes until the carrots are very tender.
Blend until very smooth. Stir in the juice of {{orange}} of oranges. Taste and season with salt. The soup should taste bright and sweet with a clear citrus edge.
Ladle into bowls. Add a spoonful of crème fraîche to each if using.

items.cause: The orange juice was added to the soup and then simmered, which drives off the volatile citrus aromatics.
items.fix: Add orange juice only after blending and off the heat. The zest added during cooking gives background flavour; the raw juice added at the end gives brightness.

items.cause: Sweet vegetable soups need acid to taste alive.
items.fix: Add more orange juice, a small squeeze of lemon, or a pinch of salt. Taste after each addition.

items.cause: Carrots and orange juice together can be very sweet.
items.fix: Add a little more lemon juice, or add a pinch of ground cumin which cuts sweetness. The crème fraîche adds a sour note that helps balance.

items.cause: Carrots require long blending to fully smooth out.
items.fix: Blend for at least 60 seconds. Pass through a fine-mesh sieve for a completely smooth, restaurant-quality finish.

content.text: Carrot and orange soup is a British creation of the 1980s, connected to the lighter style of cooking that nouvelle cuisine introduced to British restaurant menus in that decade. Pairing a sweet root vegetable with citrus to produce a light, brightly coloured soup was new at the time and quickly moved from restaurant into home cooking. By the 1990s it appeared in virtually every British general cookery book. It can also be served chilled in summer, which makes it a useful recipe for both seasons.
